content:
Guide:Walkthrough
It has been suggested that this page or section be split to Legacy:Guide:Walkthrough. [discuss]
Reason: "Finally start to clean up this page by removing information from versions almost 10 years old."
This is a Guide page.
This means the page will walk you through a specific task, strategy, or enemy/boss fight.Status: Subject to revision (This Guide does not meet certain quality standards and may be heavily outdated.)
For a simpler guide, see Guide:Game progression.
New players may also want to refer to the Getting Started Guide.
When progressing through Terraria, many players can be confused about where they should go and what they should do next. Terraria is an open-ended game: Players are not forced to go anywhere or do anything. You are free to set your own goals and follow through with them, whether you are a builder, fighter, explorer, collector, or whatever else. This walkthrough merely aims to provide a logical order of progression through Terraria's many different biomes, generally in order of increasing difficulty. It is recommended, but there is certainly no requirement to visit each biome, or even in this order if you don't want to.
When choosing a map size, consider what experience you want out of your playthrough. A small world allows for quick exploration and easier traversal of difficult biomes early on, but can also feel limited, and may be missing certain rare items. A medium world allows for longer exploration and will have more items overall, but difficult biomes will be harder to traverse early on. A large world is best suited for multiplayer, or as a world that you intend to spend a lot of time on.
Contents
1Starting Out
2Going Underground
3Events
4Building a Base
5Beyond the Forest
6The Bosses: Dealing with the first three
7The Late Pre-Hardmode
8Deerclops
9Old One's Army
10Queen Bee
11Skeletron
12Preparing For Hardmode
13Hardmode
Starting Out
On the creation of a new world, a helpful NPC (Non Player Character) will spawn, and he is called the Guide. If you are new to Terraria, then chat with him and he will give you advice on different elements of your world based on what stage of the game you're at. He can also be shown any material, and tell the player of any crafting recipes that use that item. He'll also inform the player on how to make a house that NPCs will accept as a place to live in.
Biome
Forest
New players will almost always spawn in the Forest biome.
Recommended Life
(100)
Recommended Equipment
Starting equipment
Enemies
Green SlimeBlue SlimePurple SlimeCloud SlimePinky
(rare)
Goblin Scout
(only on outer thirds of world)
Enemy Drops
GelCloudPink Gel(rare)
Slime Staff
(extremely rare)
Tattered Cloth
Notable Resources
WoodMushroomYellow MarigoldSunflowerDaybloomBlue BerriesSeed
(from mining grass with Blowpipe in inventory)
Hay(from mining grass with Sickle)Pumpkin(Halloween only)
Notable Chest Contents
Weapons:
BlowpipeGrenadeShurikenSpearThrowing KnifeUmbrellaWand of SparkingWooden ArrowWooden BoomerangFinch Staff
Accessories:
AgletClimbing ClawsGuide to Plant Fiber CordageRadarStep Stool
Other:
Can Of WormsHerb BagGlowstickRopeTorchBottleWoodCopper BarTin BarIron BarLead BarLesser Healing PotionRecall PotionBuilder PotionIronskin PotionMining PotionNight Owl PotionShine PotionSwiftness PotionSunflower MinecartLadybug MinecartWood
The landscape of a forest includes lots of grass, plenty of trees, several lakes ranging from small to large and, sometimes, Sunflowers. During the day, this biome has the weakest monsters in the game, with slow weak slimes that jump towards the player being the primary threat. Note that Goblin Scouts only spawn in the outer thirds of the map, and as such will not be encountered in the forest you start in, but can be found in other forest biomes you happen to find.
Towards the entrance of caves you'll occasionally find Surface Chests, which can contain some of the above weapons, accessories, wood, metal bars, as well as various consumables. You can also run into Living Trees, massive trees made of solid blocks, which can lead underground and contain rooms with the unique Living Wood Chests and Living Loom, as well as additional Surface Chests.
Beginning
Your goal during the day should be to find/make some better starting equipment from your surroundings and build a small house to pass the night safely. The Guide will explain how you can make a valid NPC housing. It might be wise to build multiple houses in advance to house later NPCs. NPCs will show up when you meet the conditions for them to move in (for instance the Merchant will arrive once you have collected 50 or more Silver coins), and will leave come nightfall if you haven't made them a place to stay (except for the Guide). Don't worry, they'll come back, they just wont stick around without a home. The Guide will give you hints as to how to get new NPCs to move in.
If you're playing Journey Mode, you'll start out with some better equipment in addition to many more powers over your gameplay experience, ranging from time control to item duplication. These are further explained in the main Journey Mode article.
The first thing players should do is cut down several trees (using your Copper Axe, aim for the lowest block), as wood is needed for many things, then build a Work Bench. From there, craft a Wooden Sword, and perhaps a Wooden Bow, (alongside some Wooden Arrows which players can soon upgrade to Flaming Arrows for extra damage).
Next, players should explore their surroundings. Use the Ropes you can find and Platforms you can craft to help get around your world. Ropes can be used to safely descend down further than you can build, as simply placing rope on existing rope will cause it to extend downward. If you happen to find the Umbrella tool, it allows for slow falling, which is another way to easily descend long falls. There are usually some Chests and Pots near the entrances of caves, which will often contain useful weapons, potions and materials (listed in the above "Forest" section). You should collect these chests after you loot them, as they offer additional storage space.
If you're lucky, you’ll find some of the more notable beginner weapons, such as the Wand of Sparking or Spear. These weapons will outclass your starting gear, and can be very helpful early on. You'll also likely find Recall Potions, which allow you to teleport back to your spawn. If you run into the Snow biome, you can very easily upgrade The Wand of Sparking into the Wand of Frosting, only requiring 99 Ice Torches, which can also be used to craft Frostburn Arrows for your bow. You may also run into your Evil biome, which is quite dangerous to you right now so it's likely a good idea to simply Recall Potion away at the sight of an enemy. However, while you certainly shouldn't explore them at this point, you can swiftly chop a couple trees down before recalling away and use the wood to craft either the Shadewood Sword and Shadewood Bow, or Ebonwood Sword and Ebonwood Bow for a quick and easy upgrade to your wooden weapons.
While exploring cave entrances, be sure to gather 20 stone, as combining it with some wood and torches allows you to craft a Furnace, which is required to smelt ores into bars. You can also use 5 bars of Iron or Lead to craft an Anvil, which is required to make most metal items. These two, along with your Work Bench, will be the 3 primary crafting stations you use to make the vast majority of items at this stage of the game.
After a little over 11 minutes, night will arrive. If possible, its much more productive to head underground during the night, but if you perish or simply cannot make it to a cave in time, you can wait the night out if you like.
Event
Night
Duration
9 in-game hours (9 real minutes)
Recommended Life
(100)
Recommended Weaponry
Wooden Bow
(with Flaming Arrows)
,Spear,Throwing Knife,ShurikenGrenadeWand of Sparking
Enemies
Demon EyeZombieRaven(Halloween only)
Enemy Drops
LensShackleZombie ArmWooden ArrowBlack Lens
Notable Resources
Fallen Star
Night falls at 7:30 PM, exactly 11 minutes and 15 seconds after you enter your world for the first time. It is signified by a change in music. Slimes will stop spawning and those that are left from the daytime will become hostile, seeking out players to attack. In addition, two new enemies will spawn: Demon Eyes, a flying enemy which chases players, and Zombies, which will follow a basic pattern of slowly walking towards the player in hordes.
Night has multiple effects on gameplay:
Fallen Stars will randomly fall from the sky with a glowing animation, and can be picked up by the player and crafted into a variety of things. Note that these disappear after sunrise.
Moonglow will bloom.
Some Town NPCs only sell certain items at night, such as the Merchant selling Glowsticks.
Town NPCs will remain in their houses and no new ones will move in until day arrives.
Two additional peaceful events can occur at Night. The starfall has a 1 in 10 chance of occurring each night and can be identified by stars being seen falling in the background. It's accompanied by an increased Fallen Star rate ranging from a 200% to 400% increase, as well as naturally spawning Enchanted Nightcrawler critters, which make for great bait early on (caught with a bug Net bought from the Merchant). Lantern Nights, which feature lanterns floating up into the sky in the background, will also occur the night after defeating a boss or event for the first time, increasing the player's Luck.
If you find yourself outside without a base to hide in or a cave to go down, you can simply build a box around yourself to hide for the night. A two-block wide hole on either side of the box should be enough for you to attack Zombies and gain some starter gear and money, but you can also simply choose to dig straight down, blocking the entrance of the hole to prevent zombies from following, and continuing to dig hoping to run into a cave so you can spend the night mining, as it's much more productive.
As a relevant sidenote: If you're not used to playing Sandbox building/mining games, it is easy to forget your greatest ability: the power to move blocks. If you're in a pickle, this can save you much more often than it can't.
Fallen Stars are some of the most useful crafting materials at the beginning of the game. The most immediate weapon upgrade Fallen Stars make available is the Enchanted Boomerang, made with a single Fallen Star and a Wooden Boomerang. 5 Fallen Stars can be used to craft a Mana Crystal, which increases the player's maximum mana by 20 up to maximum attainable base mana of 200. This allows the player to use magic weapons for sustained periods of time, which can be very helpful if you found a Wand of Sparking. They can also be turned into Jester's Arrows, arrows with high base damage that fly straight and pierce through crowds. Against single targets, both Flaming Arrows and Frostburn Arrows do more damage thanks to the debuffs they apply. They're also much cheaper. It's reccomended you save Jester's Arrows specifically for crowds, such as against crowds of Zombies or during events like the Blood Moon. Piercing weapons at this stage of the game are pretty rare, and if you find yourself in a situation where you need them and don't have them, it can easily create a problem, so try and save them if you can.
There are some valuable mob drops you can find at night. Zombies have a 2% chance of dropping the Shackle, which provides +1 defense. They can also drop the Zombie Arm, but only at a 0.4% chance. Demon Eyes will regularly drop the Lens, a material which has a variety of uses.
Once the sun rises and morning arrives at 4:30 AM, Night will end, causing Demon Eyes and Zombies to flee and the day to begin once again. At this point you'll be able to explore the world more, so ideally your routine should be heading underground during night and exploring in the day until you become powerful enough to ignore the day and night cycle and traverse when you please.
Going Underground
For a more in-depth guide on mining, see Mining Techniques.
Biome
Underground
Recommended Life
(100)
Recommended Weaponry
Iron or Lead Broadsword,Iron or Lead Bow,Spear,Wooden Yoyo,Throwing Knives,ShurikensGrenadesWand of Sparking
Notable Resources
Life CrystalBlinkrootGlowing MushroomMushroom Grass Seeds
Notable Chest Contents
Weapons:
Flare GunShurikenThrowing KnifeMace
Accessories:
Band of RegenerationCloud in a BottleHermes BootsShoe SpikesClimbing Claws
Other:
Magic MirrorExtractinatorAngel StatueRopeBombIron BarLead BarSilver BarTungsten BarWooden ArrowTorchLesser Healing PotionGravitation PotionFeatherfall PotionShine PotionNight Owl PotionSwiftness PotionArchery PotionGills PotionHunter PotionMining PotionDangersense PotionRecall Potion
Enemy Drops
GelPink GelSlime Staff
(extremely rare)
GlowstickJellyfish Necklace
(rare)
Whoopie Cushion
(rare)
Suspicious Looking Apple
Enemies
Giant WormBlue SlimeRed SlimeYellow SlimeBlack SlimePinky
(rare)
Blue Jellyfish
(in water)
The Underground can be identified by its background and music. It is the first layer below the surface. Copper/Tin and Iron/Lead deposits can be found in this layer as well as Silver/Tungsten and Gold/Platinum, though infrequently.
The short depth of this layer (particularly on small worlds), combined with its limited rewards, means you will probably not be spending too much time here before moving on to the more lucrative Caverns. Copper/Tin is highly abundant, but players can easily skip them by farming cactus or ebon/shadewood equipment, or even simply going with nothing more than surface chest loot until they get enough Silver/Tungsten or Gold/Platinum that they can craft equipment out of that instead due to the former ores being notably weak. However, due to the lack of range and the slow speed of the Copper Pickaxe, it's likely worth investing in a Iron/Lead Pickaxe just for the increased efficiency.
While exploring the Underground (and, by extension, the Caverns), you might find Life Crystals: you can mine and use them to increase your maximum life by 20 (up to a maximum of 400). You can also stockpile Life Crystals and use them to craft Heart Lanterns, which increases life regeneration as long as you stay near to it. It's critical that you increase your max life, as it significantly boosts your survivability. That said, your first few crystals will also unlock various events: The first allows Blood Moons to happen, and event where spawnrates are greatly increased, and more powerful enemies spawn on the surface. The third allows Slime Rains, which causes slimes to fall from the sky. If you kill 150 slimes during this event, it causes the King Slime to spawn, who is the first boss. Using a fifth and getting up to 200 HP fufills one of the conditions for the Eye of Cthulhu to spawn naturally.
It is in this layer that the player can start to encounter Underground Cabins. These cabins will contain Golden Chests, which can contain a wide variety of useful equipment, such as Hermes Boots, which allow the player to run much more quickly, or a Cloud in a Bottle, which allows the player to double jump. Along with various potions and bars. These are the main things one should be looking out for during underground exploration.
Biome
Cavern
Recommended Life
(160)
Recommended Weaponry
Iron/Shadewood Broadsword or equivalent, Iron/Shadewood Bow or equivalent, Wooden Yoyo or any of the surface Chest weapons.
Enemies
Black SlimeMother SlimeGiant WormSkeletonCave BatTimLost GirlPinkyCochineal BeetleBlue JellyfishPiranhaWall CreeperUndead Miner/Vampire Miner
And only two of these three enemies can spawn in any given world:
SalamanderCrawdadGiant Shelly
Enemy Drops
Weapons:
Slime StaffBone SwordChain KnifeRally
Tools:
BombDynamiteBone Pickaxe
Armor:
Ancient Iron HelmetAncient Gold HelmetMining HelmetMining ShirtMining PantsWizard Hat
Accessory:
Jellyfish NecklaceCompassDepth MeterMetal Detector
Other:
GelWhoopie CushionSuspicious Looking AppleHookSkullBone Vial of BloodPink Gel
Notable Resources
Dart TrapBoulderExplosivesEnchanted SwordTerragrim
Notable Chest Contents
Underground Chest contentsLava CharmWolf Fang
The Caverns can be identified by their background. It is the second layer below the surface and is by far the largest pre-hardmode biome. The Caverns are divided into two halves. In the top half, lakes of water are found, while in the bottom half, lava is found instead. Caves of Glowing Mushrooms can also be encountered.
The Caverns are a massive biome with many useful items and treasures, so they should be explored for a long time. The main things to be looking for are once again Underground Cabins, in addition to the now more common Gems and higher tier ores. The first gems the player finds should be used on making a grappling hook, as they greatly aid mobility. The player will also likely accrue a lot of money while down here, especially if they hold onto things they intention of selling them.
Although its monsters can be dangerous for a new player, the greatest threat in this layer may be the danger of falling into its deep chasms, frequently due to being hit by bats. Should you find yourself falling to death frequently, don’t forget there are a number of items that can break your fall: Cloud in a Bottle's (or equivalents) extra jump, Grappling any surface (with a Grappling Hook), including the ground you’re about to hit, or even an Umbrella. Pools of water (1+ block deep) and Cobwebs will also save you if you’re lucky enough to fall into them.
This ever-present danger is closely followed by the devious and near-invisible traps sprinkled throughout the dark caves. A bold, fearless new player skipping heedlessly through the caves for the first time will quickly meet with a Boulder landing on their head, a poison Dart Trap shooting at them, or a landmine powerful enough to destroy the surrounding terrain. Needless to say, tread with extreme caution, and take advantage of the Dangersense Potion. Upon spotting any trap or activating a Dart Trap, be sure to mine either the trap itself or the Pressure Plate. If one has no Dangersense Potions, they can also simply keep any item that uses wiring, such as the afformentioned Dart Traps or Pressure Plates in their Hotbar, as holding it will allow one to see wiring, which gives away where the traps are.
Of course, the easiest, and likely best method of avoiding the falls, the traps, and even the enemies is to use generous amounts of light sources. Torches are obviously the most common source of light, but there are alternatives. Items such as the Mining Helmet, Magiluminescence, and Shine Potions, all provide light directly on top of the player, whereas items like the Flare Gun and Glowstick offer a source of ranged light. The former three provide supreme convenience, as with light constantly directly on top of you, you never have to worry about not being able to see, whereas the latter 2 can allow one to light up an area without having to approach. All of them also allow one to see underwater, where torches will not work.
While alternate light sources certainly can aid in easing exploration, all but the Magiluminescence and the Mining Helmet are temporary, making torches still useful for revisited areas. The Mining Helmet, while permanent, is a weak gear choice and best replaced when acquiring a new set of armor for that armors set bonus. For those wanting to avoid placing torches, the Magiluminescence provides ample light, and unlike the Mining Helmet, is very useful and remains viable for much of the early game due to its acceleration bonus, a rare trait among mobility accessories. This acceleration boost is especially helpful in the tight Cavern spaces where other Movement Accessories, such as the Hermes Boots, struggle to build up speed. Even better, the Magiluminescence is craftable pre-boss with just 5 Topaz and 12 Demonite/Crimtane Bars (36 ore); the ore even glows, making it easy to find. It’s an excellent early-game upgrade, and if you're a new player struggling underground, crafting the Magiluminescence is highly recommended. The ore required to craft it is either purple (Demonite), or black and red (Crimtane). These ores require either bombs, or an upgraded pickaxe to mine (at least silver/tungsten). It is highly recommended to gather as much of these ores as you can, as they can also be used to craft powerful weapons like the Demon Bow or Blood Butcherer.
While exploring, the Skeleton Merchant may show up. He sells a plethora of useful things, such as Glowsticks, Torches, Spelunker Glowsticks, surface chest loot like the Wand of Sparking, and Yoyo-related items such as the Red Counterweight. He may also sell Roller Skates, a powerful mobility item. The roller skates allow the player to move extremely quickly, or slowly drift forwards, which is helpful for placing long rows of platforms. However, some players may find them awkward to use, due to their slippery controls.
Note that should you descend too far, you will actually find yourself in the Underworld. This will be marked by a change in music and background, along with the presence of Ash Blocks. The Underworld is extremely dangerous at this stage, and has nothing to offer yet aside from rare enemy drops.
There are many helpful tools to be found, ores to be mined, and resources to be collected in this layer. One of the first things to make is certainly a Grappling Hook simply because it is easy to craft thanks to Gem Hooks. Otherwise, the most important things to find are likely Life Crystals for reasons mentioned before, a pair of Hermes Boots, and a Cloud in a Bottle. The latter two, in conjunction with a Grappling hook and potentially a Magiluminescence, serve the important purpose of bolstering mobility when it's very much needed. Another important find would be the Magic Mirror, which can substitute as an infinite form of the Recall Potion, so you no longer need to worry about running out.
It's important to note that all of the loot listed above that is found in chests (Hermes Boots, Cloud in a Bottle, Magic Mirror) can be found elsewhere. If you cannot find any of the above listed items in the caves below the forest you spawn in, you can simply explore left or right and find an equivalent (further explained in the "Beyond the Forest" section below).
In terms of weapons a player may want to craft a Gem Staff, an upgraded Bow or Broadsword, and if they afford it, weapons out of the Evil Biome ore. There are also various weapons that can be found in the caverns either in chests or as drops from enemies, such as the Shroomerang and the Mace.
A quick note on armor: Because of set bonuses, it is wise to try and complete an armor set rather than mixing and matching different pieces. Because of how Defense works, there isn’t a huge benefit from upgrading from one armor set to the next. The Copper armor set, for example, reduces damage taken by 3, Iron armor reduces damage by 5, so the difference in damage reduction is only 2, which is barely noticeable. Yet to complete the Iron armor set requires 225 ore, which represents a significant amount of mining time. It is therefore recommended to mine Silver or Tungsten Ore for the armor because they are good enough to take on the first bosses, are significantly faster to complete than Gold or Platinum, and allows you to save the latter for Weapons and Tools. If one is worried about the lack of defense, it should be noted that the damage reduction difference between the 4 different ores is practically negligable. The circumstance in which the difference is largest is on a world with both Silver and Platinum ore, as the Defense difference between the armors is a total of seven. Even in this circumstance, while it certainly could be beneficial to get the Platinum armor, it is still mostly unnecessary.
Events
Events happen randomly as the player is traversing through the game, though some have certain criteria that need to be met in order to occur (for instance, a Blood Moon cannot occur until a player has at least 120 max health). Some events can also be summoned, and others can only be summoned and insetal will not occur randomly. These events can last either a full day/night, a random indeterminate amount of time, or will last until the player defeats them.
Event
Rain
Chance
16.6% every day (approx. every six days)
Duration
24 in-game hours (24 real minutes)
Recommended Life
(100)
Enemies
Flying FishUmbrella SlimeRaincoat Zombie
Enemy Drops
FriesRain SongUmbrella HatRain HatRain Coat
Rain occurs randomly and affects all surface biomes for its duration. Most enemies are only visually different from the normal enemies and behave exactly the same. The Flying Fish are the only one with any differences, but are still essentially a slower version of the Demon Eye. When a player is in a Snow biome during Rain, it appears as a Blizzard, though this doesn't provide any exclusive loot until Hardmode. If Rain happens at the same time as a Windy Day it appears as a Thunderstorm with exclusive music.
Effects of rain:
+20% Fishing Power while rain is active, regardless of location.
Waterleaf blooms, meaning mining it up will can also drop seeds which can be replanted.
Increased frequency of Worms in Forest biomes.
Town NPCs will remain in their houses as if it were night.
Event
Windy Day
Chance
When wind reaches 20 miles per hour
Duration
Until wind drops to or below 16 miles per hour
Recommended Life
(100)
Enemies
Angry DandelionWindy Balloon
Enemy Drops
DaybloomPaper AirplaneWhite Paper AirplaneKites
Windy Days occur when the wind speed reaches over 20 miles per hour and end when it drops to or below 16. It is indicated by background objects such as banners, trees, and vines swaying in the direction of the wind, with upbeat background music replacing the regular music. Though the event is mostly aesthetic, with objects in the background and unique NPC quotes, there are some unique changes: Angry Dandelions will sometimes spawn, firing dangerous dandelion seeds in the direction of the wind. They do heavy contact damage, but they can only attack to one side of them, making them easy to kill if the player is on the opposite side of the wind direction. Additionally, Slimes will spawn drifting on Windy Balloons. Slimes and a variety of other enemies have a small chance of dropping exclusive Kites when this event is active, which can be flown in the wind.
Ladybugs, used to craft Luck Potions, spawn in clusters frequently near towns and are very useful to catch to make farming in the game easier. Touching them (before catching them) will also give an invisible but useful luck bonus for several minutes. Be warned not to accidentally kill them with stray projectiles, as they inflict a negative luck penalty on the player.
If Rain happens at the same time as a Windy Day it appears as a Thunderstorm with exclusive music and visuals. Thunderstorms also cause lightning strikes to occur. The area lightning is about to strike can be seen from sparks emitting from that location, touching these sparks will cause them to transfer to the player, causing lightning to strike them if shelter is not found quickly. Lightning will do high damage, and on higher difficulties may outright one shot the player if their health has not been upgraded enough.
Event
Blood Moon
Criteria
1 player with at least
(120), and it's not a new moon.
Chance
Given those criteria, 1 in 9 chance (11.11%) of occurring.
Enemies
The GroomThe BrideBlood ZombieDripplerCorrupt Bunny or Vicious BunnyCorrupt Goldfish or Vicious GoldfishCorrupt Penguin or Vicious Penguin
Enemy Drops
Top HatWedding VeilWedding DressShark Tooth NecklaceMoney TroughBunny HoodSuspicious Looking EggPedguin's HoodPedguin's JacketPedguin's Trousers
Notable Resources
Corrupt Seeds or Crimson SeedsDeathweed SeedsMime MaskFallen Tuxedo ShirtFallen Tuxedo PantsVile Powder or Vicious PowderSilver BulletThrowing KnifeEvil Presence
During a Blood Moon bunnies, goldfish, and penguins will turn hostile, and the spawn rate of all monsters on the surface will increase dramatically. Additionally, enemies will be able to spawn near NPCs and Zombies can force doors open.
Deathweed will bloom, and some NPCs will also sell special wares.
If you do not wish to fight in a Blood Moon, you should probably seek shelter underground where you will be beyond its effects. But with increased danger comes increased reward. The Shark Tooth Necklace can drop from Blood Moon exclusive enemies, which increases armor penetration by 5, allowing the player to deal 2-3 extra damage to enemies with defense. The Money Trough can also drop, which allows the player to summon a flying piggy bank whenever they want, which can be helpful if the player is running low on inventory space or suddenly comes across a large sum of money.
For more on how to deal with the Blood Moon, see Guide:Blood Moon strategies.
Building a Base
A typical base
For an in-depth guide on building bases, see Guide:Bases.
Once you’ve filled your inventory with blocks and loot, you may want to consider starting to build a base, as now you have some building materials and money to buy things. A base can take anywhere from a single in-game day to more than an in-game week to complete, depending on the player's tastes. It is not necessary, however, to build the entire base right away. Waiting to build the majority of the base can be advantageous, as it will allow the player to use the large stores of blocks they will end up gathering as a by-product of mining. Regardless of when or what the player builds, it is recommended to build houses for the Guide, Merchant and Nurse, craft a Campfire, and an area for storage and crafting before progressing. It is also wise to create a layout that can be easily added onto later as more NPCs are available and more storage is needed.
You should have at least the following in your base before moving on:
Of course, houses for the Guide, Merchant and Nurse, as well as a valid empty house or two for other NPCs.
Several organized chests for item storage. You don't want to have a cluttered inventory every time you leave your base.
A campfire and sunflowers, both of which provide helpful buffs to those nearby. (Sunflowers also block the spread of Corruption and Crimson until hardmode, if you happen to have such biomes near your base.)
A crafting room, preferably with a chest or two in it to store crafting materials. Eventually, you're going to want a unified place for all your crafting needs. By now, you'll have a Work Bench, a Bottle on that Work Bench, a Chair next to it, a Furnace, an Iron/Lead Anvil, and probably a Sawmill and a Loom. A Chair and a Table adjacent to each other will serve as a crafting station for Watches. You should also leave some space for later-game crafting stations and more chests, but with just this setup, you'll be able to craft anything up to this point, from swords to robes to potions and building blocks.
If you die a lot, consider using the tombstones produced to make a flat, spread-out tree-free graveyard. If easily accessible early on it is a simple way to check regularly for Abigail's Flower which is a powerful early summon. The Ecto Mist in a graveyard is also needed to forge a Gravedigger's Shovel which can speed up digging immensely, as well as special blocks that could be useful for building interesting towns. Try to distance the graveyard slightly from the rest of the base area, as nighttime enemies can spawn at anytime in graveyards, as can other powerful enemies.
A Traveling Merchant will show up fairly early in game and sells many useful items, some of which are crucial for each class, such as the Gray Zapinator and the Code 1. He may also sell informational accessories like the Stopwatch, DPS Meter or Lifeform Analyzer. Note that the merchant will despawn if you leave the game world and depart a
